narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your family's low income bracket, and we appreciate the strength that brings to your journey. The admissions model evaluated your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 33, full-time student status, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at the 1st decile, placing you below the typical student's performance in those areas. It predicted a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection—meaning the school uses this to prioritize candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the bottom of your class that year strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Your Year 1 decile in the 1st position also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance compared to peers. On a brighter note, your low family income actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing, perhaps reflecting the resilience you've demonstrated. Your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA each provided some positive influence too, suggesting they helped offset risks but weren't enough to tip the balance. Your full-time status had a minor helpful effect, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, your profile stands out as lower than averages in key academic deciles and LSAT, which set you apart from typical admitted students who rank higher and score around 36.7 on the LSAT. This isn't a reflection of your worth or potential as a Black woman breaking barriers—it's a data-driven call based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and highlighting any resilience-building experiences from your background in a reapplication. You're capable of turning this around, and many have—keep pushing, and we believe in your path to law.
